author: @heatherstub
text: You must know about the man who went to a sayer for wisdom. The sayer introduced him to his bird call a Foo. "What? A foo?" asked the man. "Why yes." answered the old guru. "He will bring you much good luck and wisdom." On the first day, the foo plopped one on the man's right shoulder which annoyed the young man, but he cleaned it off and went about his business. On the second day, the foo plopped another dropping on his left shoulder. By now, the young man became very annoyed, thinking he must return it to the old guru. "What kind of bird could bring me wisdom and good luck if he should deficate on my shoulders?", he resentfully thought. On the third day, the foo promptly placed another turd on his right foot, at which point, the young man took the foo back to the wise, old man. "What do you mean!" he exclaimed with much anger at the old guru. "What kind of wisdom is there in a bird who decides to eliminate on my shoulders and foot?", to which the old man replied: "Ah, son. You don't see the wisdom in this situation, do you. If the foo sh*ts, wear it!"

King Shaka International Airport serves both domestic and international flights with regularly scheduled flights to Istanbul, Mauritius, London, Dubai, Doha, Lusaka, Gaborone and Windhoek, as well as eight domestic destinations. The airport's current location forms part of the 'golden triangle' between Johannesburg and Cape Town, which is important for convenient travel and trade between three major South African cities. The airport has been open since 2010.

author: @biguglystick
text: If you travel to South Africa please don't pay to play with lion cubs or 'walk with lions'. Lion interactions are offered in MANY places in South Africa, but it's a terrible industry. These lions are stolen from their mothers at only days old and then forced to be held and bottle fed by tons of humans. These lions then grow a little bigger, and when they are too big to hold they are offered as "Walking With Lions" tours... you pay to walk with them and they are prodded by sticks. Then when they grow too big to be safe with tourist interaction the lions are all sold off to CANNED HUNTING farms. This is the heinous and unforgivable practice of shooting tame, often-drugged lions in small enclosures where they have NO chance to escape or survive, then they are carted off to be a shameful "trophy" on someone's wall. A horrific thing, these lions are exploited and used from the day they are born to the day they die. I am on a mission to #RaiseAwareness about #CannedHunting and to #BanTrophyHunting in Africa and the world. Please never pay to play with lions. Lion interactions = BAD. Be an ethical tourist. #EthicalTourism. Read more at http://www.cannedlion.org/scotland.html Or see the movie #BloodLions http://www.bloodlions.org/the-film/

author: @hishramcifford4525
text: Great video! I also enjoyed your Delta One flight review. As a Canadian who moved to Johannesburg 1.5 years ago I just wanted to share a few things. You did overreact with the tap water. South Africa has some of the cleanest tap water in the world that wont make you sick. The South African constitution even has access to tap water as a basic right. I'm able to fill up my water bottle at random taps on the street. Safety is always a priority regardless of where in the world you travel to. In Johannesburg you should always be vigilant of your surroundings. There are areas that are safer than others but good judgement alone should suffice. Townships are amendment and the same goes for poverty. South Africa is the worlds most unequal country with the wealth divide being clearly visible. I noticed that your footage was filmed in wealthier areas such as Rosebank and Sandton. In your future visits I would suggest visiting more townships and areas within Joburg. Johannesburg is very large city filled with many different groups and cultures. If you like to eat and drink than South Africa is perfect! I put on weight in the first 10 months I was here simply because food is delicious and cheap. South Africa has the worlds cheapest alcohol prices and the nightlife here is incredible! Keep in mind that maximize your experience you should carry around Rands. For the most part restaurants and shops through out the country will accept credit cards but it's not uncommon to find street vendors and even some stores not accepting cards with American Express being the least accepted. There are no Sanctions in South Africa that i'm aware off but the weak GDP and rapid inflation has lead to a weaker rand. The only benefit of a weak Rand is how much you receive when converting US Dollars. For safety reasons I would suggest not carrying a lot of cash on you or you'll be a target for muggings. What most people notice when visiting South Africa is the abundance of ATM's. You don't have to go far to find an ATM in South Africa. When I first came to South Africa I was surprised to see so many luxury vehicles. A few reasons for this are: (1) A lot of German cars are manufactured in South Africa, (2) Large economic inequality within the nation with a lot of money being inherited family money, (3) High income professionals such as Doctors, Lawyers, Business people, etc.. benefit from the wealth inequality and cost of living. I see a lot of Doctors here driving $100,000+ cars while Canadian Doctors drive Honda's. Along with the division in wealth certain businesses thrive by tailoring their services more to those with high incomes. In summary, South Africa is a beautiful country that I hope you return to very soon. Try and visit other cities next time such as Pretoria, Durban, Nelspruit, Sun City, and the list goes on. If you return to Johannesburg again please let me know and if I'm still around we can link up and i'll show you some more gems of both the city and possibly townships. All the best and continue sharing your adventures. I've both liked and subscribed as i look forward to more content.

author: @michaelkelleher862
text: Your video was interesting but does not deal well with the safety aspects. I’m from Ireland and married to a South African Zulu. Here most citizens live in their own self contained prisons. I can never go for a walk like I use to when I lived in Cape Town. I’m warned continually to be watching my security. I could be killed for my watch, mobile phone or even my shoes. The level of violence, brutality and endemic corruption is very high. The poor are extremely poor and the wealthy extremely wealthy. There is a weak middle class. In most countries where there is a strong middle class wealth percolates to the less wealthy thus giving a decent standard of living. At night time people do not stop at a traffic light on red, they could be mugged or killed. You did not visit any informal settlements. If I went to these townships or informal settlements unaccompanied by my wife and stepson I would probably not live to tell the tale. Fortunately for you you could blend in. Society here is oppressive and freedom of movement is not what it should be.

author: @rwt5713
text: My favorite place on earth. I was there for three weeks in August and I fell in love with the city. I've been travelling for a year and I'm yet to find a place that even comes remotely close to matching how amazing Cape Town was. Walking along Sea Point, having a beer at the V&A Waterfront, hiking Table Mountain, breathing in the fresh Atlantic air down in Cape Point... I really wanted to stay there forever. It's a shame it receives to much negativity due to the crime rate, but you got to remember, most of the crime takes place in Cape Flats. I've never felt safer walking around by myself day or night. And the people are so proud of their city, as they should be.

author: @briantravelman
text: I often hear people complain that the private reserves aren't really an authentic safari experience, and are often compared to safari parks or zoos, especially in areas where the animals have been reintroduced, but I'm not so sure. Maybe the animals are semi-wild and fenced off to keep them from entering the resorts and villages, but you still have to drive around and look for them and you may or may not see them. But they're usually restricted to a smaller area, which makes seeing them more likely. On the downside however, private reserves usually have a limit on how many animals they're allowed to have, so you don't get such large herds or species diversity. But IDK, the flora and fauna are still indigenous. It's true that the accommodations and getting there, can be extremely pricey by local standards, but on the upside game drives in these places are usually cheaper than in national parks. And if you visit as part of a packaged tour, you can often get a discounted rate. Some of the smaller and more popular ones are reasonable. Personally, I think if you're staying in Cape Town, just visit one of the reserves. But if you're staying in Jo-berg or PE, you're better off just visiting a national park. That being said, South Africa is one of the only African countries that caters to people who want to experience more than just safari. Most other African countries seem to be centered entirely around safari tourism, and if you want to experience other things, it becomes a pricey and logistical nightmare. At least in SA, you're able to do both without having to pay ridiculous amounts of money or bounce from hotel to hotel.

author: @SonjaEggers
text: The Kruger National Park is self-destructing! Sadly with over 40 000 elephants in the Kruger National Park today the park is on an irreversible self-destruction downward spiral. Noways people will say. Well, then go onto Google Earth Pro and search for the Aruba Lodge in Tsavo Kenya. Zoom in and see the vegetation around the fenced off lodge. That is what the vegetation looked like 60 years ago all around the lodge, and for miles beyond. Evergreen, impervious to fire and dense such that visibility was restricted to a mere 50 meters according to Ian Parker. The elephant population grew and grew to an estimated 40 000, a density of one elephant for every 55 hectares, progressively transforming the surrounding terrain from a woodland to a grassland that now became prone to fires. In the process the burgeoning elephant population, besides depleting the nutritional value of the vegetation, removed the browsing food for the black rhino that used to number 8 000 in Tsavo. The black rhino started to die off of hunger, in addition to being subjected to poaching. It is now extinct there - extirpated! Besides the black rhino other browsers like the lesser kudu and bushbuck also suffered and perished. Then in 1970 a two year drought presented itself resulting in a vegetation collapse and with it some estimated 15 000 elephants died of malnutrition, not poaching. Now zoom out from the Aruba Lodge on Google Earth Pro and see what desert Tsavo now presents from having been an evergreen thicket with nothing left but dust and barren landscapes. The Kruger Park, similar in size at 2 million hectares, with an elephant density of one elephant for every 46 hectares is now, after 29 years of a hands-off management approach, halfway to reaching the same environment as Tsavo experienced those decades ago. An adult elephant consumes around 150 kgs of biomass each and every day. In addition to that it destroys a further roughly 55 kgs in its wasteful feeding habits so a minimum of 210 kgs per day or 76 tonnes of biomass are removed from the KNP each year. Allowing for 15% being calves that do not consume as much, the high density of elephants in the KNP remove 2,8 million tonnes of biomass each year and that is growing in line with the 6.5% annual growth rate of the elephant population. Now factor in droughts and fires and the Tsavo spectre is in your face. The Kruger Park would have had, in its day, the highest diversity in all of Africa - but this no more. This is irreversible. Strategies of closing water holes have had a zero impact on the elephant growth rate. The corridors and transfrontier park area is north of the Olifants River while the bulk of the elephant population is south of this. An elephant is loyal to its home range and will not leave it unless it has to do so. That will only come about then when the nutritional value of the vegetation in its home range has been so depleted so that it is forced to look for greener pastures elsewhere. The tragedy of this is that this will happen long after all the other species that need tall large trees for nesting and habitat have disappeared because their habitat has been destroyed just as happened in Tsavo over half a century ago.

author: @TryangleFilms-t8v
text: As black people watching this portal into the past, historical contexts aside, none of us can deny that Joburg in the 70's LOOKED a lot better than it does now. Cause nowadays, unless you're rich and can afford to live a more affluent area of the city, Joburg is not fit for human habitation. It sucks on just about every conceivable level. And I hate that because I'm black, I have to pretend that that's not the case. That it's "not so bad." Is this what it's come to? That we have to defend a toxic hell-hole of a city because it's governed by people with the same skin tone as us? People, I might add, who couldn't give two fucks about you or your opinion of their competency. People who would sell you to the highest bidder (which they do often btw) at the drop of a hat. Is this the best we can do as black people? Are we so defiant in our struggle with our own shortcomings that we can't even acknowledge that something merely LOOKED better in the past?

author: @ZionaLotz
text: This isn't about the old regime of apartheid, this was when we were proud of our country. Back then we maintained our infrastructure and we had public servants in the public sector that actually did what they were paid for. It was not an "Afrikaner" thing,I have an English Father and Afrikaans mother. Everybody did their part, the streets were clean, we had a railway system, no power outages, the death penalty, SAA was rated as one of the top airways. Corruption, mismanagement and greed has brought us where we are today, certainly not Apartheid although that word has become the scapegoat for just over 30 years. What a waste.

And something similar is happening here in Brazil; corruption is rampant, there's a lack of basic sanitation, and most of my city's territory is controlled by gangs. This reminded me of the times of the Brazilian Empire, when despite the problems, things were much better than today, because since the proclamation of the republic, it's been all downhill. author: @thandalion6313
text: After years of watching wildlife programmes Kenya and Tanzania were my first choice for safari and I wasn't disappointed. After that I did a safari in Zambia on the lower Zambezi and Kafue NP. My next visit to Africa was a 4 week conservation project in South Africa. East Africa: The savannah with umbrella acacia is always the mental picture I hold when I think of Africa. The wide open plains make it easier to spot the animals but a downside is that vehicles are not allowed to leave the designated tracks so the animals could be some distance away from your vehicle. One issue with the Serengeti and Masai Mara is the number of safari vehicles. It was easy to spot where the more elusive animals were because they were usually surrounded by ten or more safari vehicles. I enjoyed the cultural aspects. We spent time with Masai and Hadzabi tribes and it was definitely interesting learning about their cultures. However, although they were very welcoming it did feel a little intrusive, particularly with the Hadzabi. A major downside for me is that my body does not react well to anti malarials. Although I tried different types for both East Africa and Zambia, on each occasion I was ill with diarrhoea from about day three and for a week or more after I returned home. Most of East Africa is a malarial zone so going without medication is a risk not worth taking. Tsetse flies were a nuisance in the Serengeti. Apart from the risk of sleeping sickness that they carry, their bites are very painful. South Africa: I spent 4 weeks on a private game reserve (PGR) in Kwazulu Natal. The big plus for me is that it is classed as malaria free or low risk so I needed no anti malarials and had absolutely no stomach issues for the whole of my stay. As you say, instead of the wide open savannah the terrain is quite dense bush. Most animal sightings were around watering holes and/or clearings. The PGR I stayed at did not have metalled roads like the Kruger but I believe the terrain is very similar but there were not dozens of safari vehicles. Unlike the Kruger the leopards were very elusive and despite doing game drives about 8 hours each day I did not see a single leopard in the whole of the 4 week stay. We lived and worked alongside members of the Zulu tribe and I really enjoyed my time with them without that feeling of intrusiveness. Zambia: The terrain is more like SA, particularly in Kafue NP. We did a canoe safari on the lower Zambezi and I would definitely recommend that to anyone. When I saw it on the itinerary it almost put me off the Zambia trip but it actually turned out to be the best part of the holiday for me, despite the dangers of hippos and crocodiles. Kafue is among some of the last truly wild game parks. It also offers guided game walks and night time safaris which I also enjoyed. Kafue had a huge number of Tsetse flies which made game drives in an open top vehicle a very painful experience. I would love to visit East Africa again but memories of my reaction to anti malarials does deter me. Both safaris involved wild bush camping in campsites with no fences to keep the animals out and with few or basic amenities. Having to get up in the early hours to dash to a toilet that has no lights is not only inconvenient but dangerous.

author: @kwaaikat100
text: Good vlog, but a note on cost is warranted.If you say South Africa offers more luxury, as it can give the impression that SA is more expensive. The reality is the polar opposite. You didn’t mention cost, and only seem to compare South African private lodges (hence the repeated use of the word luxury). But the Kruger itself (as opposed to private lodges in the “greater” Kruger) has a wide a range of accommodation, including more rustic accommodation than South African private lodges, and to say Kruger itself works out a lot cheaper than East Africa is an understatement. That is not to mention the cost and logistics of getting there. For the Kruger you can simply fly to Johannesburg, it is a relatively easy 4 hour drive to the Kruger with a rental car. You can combine self drive (which has it’s own thrill) with guided night drives to have incredible value of bang for buck. Authenticity is a bit subjective. Yes many Kruger roads are tarred, yes the infrastructure is developed. I’ve been to a few guided trips elsewhere in Africa, and you get shown an animal by the guide, it is “oh yes great”. In the Kruger if you self drive with a map, nothing beats the thrill of an unexpected sighting, an animal jumping out of the bush, that you’ve found yourself. It also means you can linger at a sighting for as long (or as short) a time as you want. Another thing not mentioned is not only the variety of options, but the variety of landscapes and habitats in the Kruger itself, let alone other reserves. The Northern Kruger is a different place compared to the Southern and Central Kruger. Each visit is different. Each season is different. That is why people return to the Kruger for their 2nd, third, foruth or 20th visit. Many people see the Serengeti as an ultimate bucket list tick item: The Kruger is more the type of place you never tire of.

author: @petrusmarais7281
text: I have lived my whole life in SA and have never been on a safari, though I have been to nature reserves many times. There's no need to pay a fortune on a safari, of course, they probably offer a unique experience, but the following works just as well. I'd suggest renting a car (if you don't own one) and just going to wherever (Kruger, Marakele, Pilansberg, Addo, so many amazing choices), with family or a couple of friends. Of course, the best would be to go along with locals, whether on a safari or with acquaintances that live here. Just do your planning well. Find out which routes you can take with the car you have. Find out where to go, most reserves have extensive maps and planning to help you out. IMPORTANT!!! Use common sense. The animals are wild, and if you have your arm hanging out the window or whatnot, don't be upset when something takes a bite. Don't be dumb, be safe. Remember that whichever park you go to, belongs to the animals. You are only visiting. So respect the owners. Finally, enjoy. No matter how many times you go, each will be unforgettable. Really finally, it's too bad the video didn't mention hiking. There are some breathtaking routes. For examples, check out the link: https://www.getaway.co.za/travel-ideas/hiking-bucketlist-south-africa/ These are some of the difficult hikes, but you will find many shorter and easier hikes in these same places and elsewhere. They are all magnificent. I haven't been everywhere on the list, but some of my favourites are: Drakensberg, God's Window, Golden Gate. Places I still NEED to go are, Mkambati, Otter, Fish River Canyon, Ceres, Tsitsikamma, Blyde River Canyon... eish... too many to list. We really are blessed with choices. Really, really finally. Nowhere in the country have I been told to not drink tap water. Tap water is safe in the majority of towns.

author: @sollyletsoalo3771
text: Our winter generally runs from May to August. As mentioned in the video SA is a big country and winters in other parts are fairly warm (eg. Durban) while other parts like Joburg can be cold. Winter temperatures in Joburg fluctuates between 5 degrees Celsius at night to around 15 degrees during the day. There is an occasional cold front that drops the temperature below 5 degrees but it only last for a few days. November is smack in the middle of summer.

author: @Tekweni
text: 🌿 A note from Tekweni — and a correction we owe you Thank you to everyone who has shared personal memories of the Garden Route in the comments — from a van trip in the early 80s, to growing up in Mossel Bay, to discovering an ancestor in John Benn. These responses remind us why we make documentaries: not just to inform, but to reconnect people with places and stories that shaped them. A sharp-eyed viewer has pointed out an inaccuracy regarding Portland Manor — the historical record shows the land was sold to Henry Barrington, who built it, not to a daughter of George Rex as stated in the video. We appreciate the correction and acknowledge that some details in this older production may not reflect the most current historical scholarship. The Garden Route's history is rich enough to deserve its own dedicated series — and that is something we are considering. 📌 Continue the journey through South Africa's past: 🎥 South African History Uncovered — Stories That Shaped a Nation 👉 https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLAleUNY7ZZrqg-enZ6Rf6DMjvSP5C8FVb

author: @Progressivethots
text: BEFORE you look at the results - what Exactly was the key Root cause 🤔 When did apethaid deny blacks basic education and time frame❓ Under Apartheid in South Africa, Black South Africans were systematically denied equal education mainly through the Bantu Education Act of 1953. The law was introduced by Hendrik Verwoerd and came into effect in 1954. It created a separate and inferior school system for Black students. Time frame 1953 — Bantu Education Act passed 1954–1994 — apartheid education policies enforced 1994 — apartheid officially ended when South Africa became a democracy under Nelson Mandela How basic education was denied Black South Africans were: Given far less funding than white schools Taught in overcrowded classrooms Denied quality textbooks and facilities Trained mainly for low-paying labor jobs instead of higher education or professional careers Restricted in subjects and opportunities available to them Verwoerd openly stated that Black people should not be educated beyond the roles apartheid assigned to them. The system was designed to keep racial inequality in place. The video you are showing took 1954 - I994 .

author: @cohenc
text: Only just come across this eye opener. Wonderful and inspiring people. I am a white westerner living in the UK. One of many thing this brings home is a sense of the scale of migration to SA from other African countries. I was aware of several million Zimbabweans but had little sense of the numbers of 'economic' migrants absorbed by SA. It put's to shame the current furore in the UK over a relatively small number of migrants attempting to enter the UK by perilous crossings of the Channel or any other means. We visited Cape Town in 2019 and did a tour of Khayelitsha led by Uthando. I like to think the visit was educational, and not voyeuristic of patronising. Conditions in Khayelitsha seemed on a level above Masiphumelele. Leads to the question what is the difference between an informal settlement and a township? I guess a township is recognised by public authorities so in theory it has access to public resources - water, electricity, sewage, etc. For a week we stayed in the western comfort of Nordhoek – it's hard to believe Masiphumelele was less than 10k away showing the stark contrasts and gaping inequality of SA. That conditions might be so much better than the slums of origin of many of the migrants does of course not excuse the situation, I realise now I cycled alone on the main road past Masiphumelele, If I am honest I recall being nervous at the time but must say I faced no hostility at all. I hope I can make this journey again and passing solid buildings that the community deserves. PS I have subscribed to AT and look forward to the journey

author: @jonathanmills5747
text: I have been to Cape Town on 3 occasions. the first exactly 30 years ago, just 6 months after Nelson Mandela became the President. I have never stopped telling friends to get down there and how amazing it is compared to anywhere else on earth I have travelled to. The people are friendly, although there's over 350 years of division that will take a few more generations to heal old attitudes as is the case in many countries were under a colonial rule from a foreign power. There is an issue with crime and when it happens to overseas tourists it makes headlines bigger than proportionally it does for example if it happens in Europe. You have to be sensible and heed advice from locals again as with any place you visit and are unfamiliar with. The cost of living in terms of food, drink, trips is surprisingly reasonable due to the low value of the Rand against the Pound Euro or US dollar. Remember that when planning a visit you need to be aware that in the Southern Hemisphere the seasons are the opposite to the Northern Hemisphere. So for the Spring and Summer in South Africa, you will find that the Autumn and Winter in the Northern Hemisphere are a good time to plan for typically warmer weather. Stellenbosch which is part of the Wine region I can only describe as the blue print of the Garden of Eden. Seriously there are other areas of South Africa which are as beautiful but my personal favourite example is there. If you like fish and seafood, you will think you have died and gone to Heaven, ask for recommendations at any seafood restaurant and don't be afraid to try something new, often species you may never of heard of. The Capetonians really know their stuff when it comes to culinary expertise. And the choices are so fresh, we're not talking about frozen processed fish fingers or preserved styles. If you like to eat fish, then you will be returning to the Cape more than once in your lifetime and end up boring your friends with stories and reasons they have to go and see for themselves, like I have done.

text: Hey friends — if you want all my Cape Town recs, here’s a free guide I made for you: https://bit.ly/CapeTown-Guide-Camden Feel free to watch this for 26 more tips for Cape Town (new and updated): https://youtu.be/zp39bOXH3Ps

author: @Truthseeker371
text: Loadshedding is not the government conserving the electricity. It's mismanagement caused by the corrupted politicians and bureaucrats. In SA, you prepay for the electricity. Many users don't pay up the bill.

author: @laurabezden1874
text: I’m an American who lived in SA from 89 to 2001. I travel back often. This video has some valid points, but I have found that most videos are made by younger people with a different idea of what is “good” than an older person. I’m 68. I used uber for the first time there exclusively (didn’t have a car) and would not recommend. Maybe because I didn’t used the ultra uber, but I found most of them dirty and unpleasant. I would not stay in Seapoint. Dirty and very loud and not the safest. Gardens is the best of what he recommended. I’ll make a video at some point for older people. However, S A is wonderful and everyone should visit.

author: @RobertScott1952
text: Thanks for the video. Well done. I live in Cape Town and can recommend everything he said. I would add (1) Do NOT climb Table Mountain on your own. It is a dangerous place - always tell someone exactly where you intend to hike and hike in a group. (2) The suburbs he mentioned are all fine for a tourist. There are however areas that are "no go"s, ask the locals first. (3) There is cold water on the Atlantic side (Camps Bay etc) and warm water in the Indian Ocean side (Muizenberg and Fish Hoek) which would be better advised for swimming, in summer obviously.

author: @TravelTrailsTastes
text: I have to say — you nailed it! I'm a Capetonian in South Africa. author: @sam-the-nomad
text: I would add a safety tip from personal experience – I found that there are quite a lot of people who persistently beg and follow you down the street if you are walking alone. If it happens, just ignore them and most of them are harmless, but don't turn down a street that's a bit quiet and offer any easy opportunities. It happened to me in Cape Town. They started bothering me as I exited the supermarket (Spar in Cape Quarter for those who are familiar) and then I ignored and went one block down the street and they followed me. Suddenly the street was very quiet and they took the opportunity to try and rob the contents of my backpack. Luckily a minibus taxi just happened to drive by and the driver very kindly helped scare him away. I don't mean to scaremonger, because despite this, it's still one of my favourite cities in the world. Also, based on the reaction I got from locals when I told them my experience, they admitted that these things can happen but they said it was quite uncommon.

author: @kennekam
text: Small correction. Bobotie is a Cape Malay dish, also part of traditional Afrikaans cuisine. Seldom see it outside the Western Cape. So not our national dish. Problem is that every culture in the country has its own favourite dish. But what is the common popular dish is barbecued meat and some sort of starch, primarily maize meal. Various names such as braai en pap, shisanyama, etc.

author: @CanadaMath
text: The country of South Africa 🇿🇦 was run by white people until 1990. They had developed it into almost first world country status. The infrastructure, economy, railways, airline were ranked the best in Africa. Famous singers would go there to do concerts. Then in 1990 the country handed over ruling power to the blacks in the ANC (African National Congress). Since then the country has been destroyed. South Africa airlines is now bankrupt. The railway is abandoned. 90% of the farms have failed. South Africa now has the highest unemployment rate in the world and second highest murder rate in the world. The country can not even provide basic utilities to their people. Most white people have left and now make up only 7% of the population. Lesson to be learned: never give your country to blacks. P.S. It is important to note that the black South Africans (The Bantu) are NOT native to that land. They moved there from East Africa because they were escaping Islamic persecution. The only humans who are truly native to South Africa are the 'San Bushmen'. Interestingly they do not live there anymore, as they moved to Namibia and Botswana many decades ago. White European settlers came initially from the Netherlands, and then later Germany, France and Great Britain. It is these people and their descendants that built the nation of South Africa. The Bantu built absolutely nothing. They only destroyed what the white South Africans had developed.

author: @glenmoepi9635
text: I got the 2012 F30 320i, 8 years ago, the car did not even reach 50 000 KM, Drivetrain error after just 30 000KM of ownership, Bmw tried to fix it with their best Technicians to no avail, dey changed fuel systems and all but the problem was never solved for almost 6months. Luckily the car was still under motorplan and the dealership borrowed me 520D, very comfortable but not reactive to drive like 320i. After failing to solve the problem, they settled my debt and gave me F30 320D, 2016 model, eight years down da line now at 200 000KM, the car neva broke down. Just normal regular and major services, tyres and dats it, 3months ago I did Pretoria to Thohoyandou and back 950Km in one day. No issues...in one full tank n still had enuf to go fo work for the whole week. Diesel engines are built to last.

author: @johnsetlogelo
text: car ownership experience & mechanical knowledge goes a long way if you want to buy & keep a high mileage vehicle, also you mustn't be stingy when it comes to parts. in 2022 i needed a vehicle proper ground clearance & had to be 4x4 but i was not interested in getting car finance, so i sold my 2016 golf 7 r & bought a 2010 disco 4 hse with 190k km at we buy cars midstream. first thing i did after the purchase was a major service, including geabox & diff oils, also replaced transfer case & both front air struts. afterwards the disco hasn't put a foot wrong, it has taken me & the family 4 times to the south coast; botswana; lesotho; eswatini; namibia & all over south africa, it just keeps on chowing the kms, no matter how many you throw at it. currently its at 350k km & i'm looking forward to many more happy travelling days. ✊long live high mileage diesel cars, long live✊

author: @jerrybuirski2344
text: Apart from Ubers you can also get Bolt and we use both of these. Self-drive is easy, especially outside the big cities, and non-UK or Commonwealth visitors just need to get used to driving on the left. As a Capetonian, I can never figure out just why anyone would willingly visit Johannesburg, but there you go, whatever floats your boat. It is a pity you didn't know about the District Six Museum in Cape Town, as it is very good and extremely moving. You might have noticed the large open area slightly southeast between the city and Table Mountain, which is where the multicultural and mostly Coloured District Six used to be before being bulldozed by the Apartheid regime. One of the many stories in the museum is about a former house-holder who kept pigeons, and some time after being forced to move to the Cape Flats, outside the city, his pigeons didn't come home one day. On a whim he decided to drive back to District Six, and there on the razed plot where his house used to be were all the pigeons sitting on the ground, being like "What did you do to the house?"

author: @Dinosauronthefloor
text: More tips - driving a 4×4 is a wise choice as it means you'll be able to travel through more roads and the risk of getting stuck is less likely 🚗 - you can buy food from the Kruger shops, but they are more expensive, try to get a cooler and stock it from a grocery store outside the Kruger and then store it properly once you get to your bungalow. - know the signs of an upset elephant, what they look like right before they're going to charge at you. Do not drive close to them and stay slow around them when passing them. - wild dogs may chew and puncture your tyres if you stop your car for too long near them.

author: @anitadeclerck6000
text: I've been living in the Northern Suburbs of Cape Town my whole life. Our water has always been safe to drink, summer is the windy months, so I suggest to visit us in May. We also get winter rain, whereas the other provinces get summer rain. Next time try going up the West Coast, and you will be pleasantly surprised. You also have to experience a proper braai from beginning to end. All South Africans do it the same and insist on a "braai broodjie". A bit of an insult to say a braai is like a bbq. Thanks for sharing your experience.

text: 16. When it comes to clothing, rather buy it here. Do bring a warm set of comfortable clothing, houses here are not made for the cold but on warm to hot days, your european will just be too hot. We have very large shopping centers here and you will be able to find everything you need.

author: @tanyabethell26
text: Glad you enjoyed your visit here and it's good to make videos like this for other travellers, however, you didn't visit South Africa you visited the Western Cape. The unpredictable weather and wind really only pertains to the Western Cape and the Eastern Cape provinces. KwaZulu Natal is sub-tropical and has quite a different climate, Limpopo and Mpumalanga have great weather, no wind and are consistent generally. The province with the best weather is Gauteng. So certainly pack different clothing but it also depends where one is going in South Africa. I hope you visit again and see other parts of the country which in my opinion are far more beautiful and interesting then the Western Cape. Happy holidaying 😊

author: @soara4634
text: And the mosquitoes will leave you alone if you drink gin and tonic. Over time it has become a kind of myth people jokingly toss about, but we just spent two weeks in the Lowveld this New Years, right on the shore of the Vygeboom dam which is a near stagnant body of water in the heart of Malaria territory, and I didn’t get a single mosquito bite. Drank almost exclusively gin and tonic. Locals don’t take preventative malaria medications because they suppress the symptoms and end up increasing your risk, and if you avoid these areas on that basis you will miss out on some of the best things like the Kruger National Park, the Blyde River Canyon, the whole of the Lowveld and the beaches of the north.

author: @amanwo89
text: Insider tips. The weather is unpredictable from November to January and often windy. From February the wind tends to slow but temperatures peak over this month with many days being 30C. This is when you want to be in Newlands which is now cool. In march, wind and temperature both ease. It is true that Newlands experiences more rain than London. This happens mainly in this small area due to the mountains. As someone who lives in London this may be true, but it definitely isn’t as overcast. Loads-shedding is no longer really a thing, but maybe check before you visit as you never know if it will become a thing again.

author: @soara4634
text: Ten to twenty Rand for a car guard? No wonder they harass us locals when we give them what WE can afford which is R2-R5. We had a lady in my neighborhood who got her car door slammed onto her legs because she didn’t have a tip for the “guard”. I can’t afford to tip car guards that kind of money, that is more than I would pay for several hours of secure undercover parking. Informal car guards take zero responsibility for your safety, won’t intervene in car theft, and might actually attack you of they are unhappy with your tip, so they are little more than beggars in discarded reflector vests. In spite of that they still make more than minimum wage even on R2’s and R5’s, tax free and with no paper trail, considering most of them are illegal immigrants. Tourists to SA, please don’t indulge this beyond the coins in your pocket. Locals also don’t tip petrol attendants because they are salaried workers.

text: Hey Harry, You should go to Spain and make a mini-documentary on squatters it’s a legal nightmare that’s spiralling out of control. Once squatters occupy a home for more than 48 hours, the owner is virtually powerless. They can’t evict them, can’t cut off electricity or water, yet they’re still forced to keep paying the mortgage, taxes, and bills on a property they no longer control. It’s now escalated to the point where entire hotels are being taken over, effectively held hostage, while owners bleed money as if the squatters were lawful residents. In response, a shadow industry has emerged private eviction firms, often run by former criminals with prison histories, hired to do what the law won’t and forcibly remove squatters.

author: @flowerfaeri
text: So my experience in Jo'burg was very different. I stayed at a BnB run by a white lady who also acted as a tour guide for a small additional fee. It felt like stepping back into the 70's but I did not go to the rough areas like you did. Nevertheless, I was told by my host of a young woman being robbed by three men outside a bank, and a couple who'd rented a car who were pulled over and robbed by fake cops. I didn't experience any problems except minor issues in some shops, where the workers wouldn't serve or acknowledge me because I'm white. Most people of both races were extremely kind and friendly. I also had a taxi-tour in Port Elizabeth with an Indian South African. It truly is a Rainbow nation. I did hear the same story about refugees from other African countries facing discrimination, and saw a white man living rough on the street in Durban (don't know his story). If you're ever in Jo'Burg, I highly recommend the Truth and Reconciliation Museum - it provides a heartbreaking and informative look into South African history - pre, during and post apartheid.

author: vagabrothers
text: How's it? We're Marko Alex the Vagabrothers and this week we're talking about Cape Town South Africa Cape Town is one of our favorite cities in the world so we are super stoked to share our top 10 things to do in Cape Town South Africa

author: vagabrothers
text: number one Table Mountain Table Mountain is the symbol of Cape Town a massive plateau overlooking the city below take the cable car up enjoy a sunset cocktail at the mountain top bar number two Lionshead for an active option hike up Lion's Head, Table Mountain's little sister

author: vagabrothers
text: this 45 minute hike is really popular with locals it's a healthy way start your trip Number three, Llandudno. Next spend the afternoon in Llandudno a gorgeous beach town with beautiful people and great waves But keep your eyes peeled because the waters are sharky.

author: vagabrothers
text: Number four, cage diving. sharks have a bad rep dispel the myths and learn about Great Whites first-hand but hopping in the water with them dives take place three hours from Capetown shark alley which has the highest concentration of great white sharks in the world

author: vagabrothers
text: Number five wine tasting Capetown's Mediterranean climate makes it perfect for cultivating wine especially South Africa's own Pinotage grape many wineries have been around for centuries and most offer tastings paired with incredible food Number six Chapman's Peak Road

author: vagabrothers
text: take the long way back to Cape Town with a drive down the scenic Chapman's Peak road home to some of the most gorgeous coastline in South Africa for extra fun and style points rent a ride and do it in a motorcycle side car Number seven The Old Biscuit Mill

author: vagabrothers
text: grab lunch at the old biscuit mill a renovated space where artisan food stalls serve top quality cuisine to a stylish and beautiful crowd it's quite "lekker my china" Number Eight nightlife when night falls it's time to grab a drink downtown

author: vagabrothers
text: now most to the bars are located on Long Street but less touristic option is just two streets over on Bree Street Number Nine Mzoli's Place Downtown Cape Town's nice but to experience more of the city head to Mzoli's Place in the township of Guguletu

author: vagabrothers
text: for Sunday Braai, it's basically a backyard barbecue meet's a street party and trust us if there's one experience you gotta have it's a Sunday braai at Mzoli's Place Number Ten Robben Island for a more sobering experience

author: vagabrothers
text: pay a visit to Robben Island where Nelson Mandela was imprisoned from 1964 to 1990 it's a good place to learn about the history of apartheid and South Africa's continuing struggle for racial and social justice well those are our recommendations on Cape Town

author: vagabrothers
text: what are yours if you're from there or you've been before make sure that you leave your tips and advice in the comment box below Also if you haven't seen our previous videos from Cape Town there'll be a link in the end boards right after this so as always

author: iflymagazine
text: We have so much. There's the mountains, the beaches, we have more of the city life... ...like five different countries all within walking distance. My name is Nicki, I am a designer and I've got two children. We live in Hout Bay. I have a homeware company called Love Milo.

author: iflymagazine
text: Love Milo is a homeware design and deco brand... ...that uses only sustainable materials... ...and is nature-inspired. Here we're in Cape Town, standing on Signal Hill... ...looking over the Cape Town CBD, which is the central area of Cape Town... ...the belly of Table Mountain.

author: iflymagazine
text: What I love most about Cape Town is the diversity of the people. It's a huge variety of different people that live here, from a very large range of cultures. Everybody just kind of lives together and appreciates one another's cultures. It's very special. It's cool.

author: iflymagazine
text: In Cape Town we've always been quite aware of sustainability... ...because we have so much nature around us. The food reflects the people of Cape Town... ...with its differences and uniqueness. A lot of it is local. Local produce, vegetables, fruits.

author: iflymagazine
text: A lot of very nice produce here. This is the Oranjezicht market. It's located on the residential property of the Premier of the Western Cape, Helen Zille. It actually used to be on a heritage site, so they couldn't have it there anymore.

author: iflymagazine
text: Helen Zille actually decided to have the market here. She's actually a really, really cool chick. She really is. It's cool, eh? This is definitely my favourite part of the weekend. You'll find the best produce, the most beautiful vegetables and fruits.

author: iflymagazine
text: And little special treats to use for lunch on a Saturday or Sunday. My studio's located in Woodstock. It's an area of Cape Town that has got a lot of history. It's got a lot of rough edges, a lot of beautiful street art...

author: iflymagazine
text: ...but also a lot of new trendy art studios and galleries. This is one of my favourites: What If The World. We're at the Biscuit Mill, which is an old flower mill. They've converted it into various retail shops, really cool little shops, and restaurants.

author: iflymagazine
text: They have an amazing market on Saturday. We're in Kalk Bay Harbour, which is one of my best places to come on a Saturday. To come here on the weekend is a really nice thing to do with your family or your friends.

author: iflymagazine
text: There are beautiful restaurants and it's also really photogenic to come and take pictures... ...or take your kids fishing. It's is one of my favourite things to do. Behind me is one of my favourite restaurants, Harbour House.

author: iflymagazine
text: It's got an incredible view, it's literally right on the water... and the food is unbelievable. Really beautiful fresh fish... ...and unbelievable views, with the water that literally comes scratching against the window.

author: iflymagazine
text: We've seen a lot of beautiful places, starting off in Woodstock, to the market, to Kalk Bay. We're now in Bree Street at one of my favourite places to go for cocktails with my friends. It's called Orphanage. There is a ton more to see, so I would highly recommend coming here.

author: iflymagazine
text: This is just a tiny taste of what Cape Town has to offer. In Afrikaans we would say: Gesondheid.

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: Welcome to 3D Planet, I’m Katrina Hobbs. The African spirit brings to mind vast tribal cultures, amazing wildlife and unsolved mysteries. To enjoy our 3D Planet perspective, just remember to put on your 3D glasses every time you see the 3D symbol showing.

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: South Africa is a country full of contrasts. At the southern tip of the continent, you’ll discover a wide range of racial diversity. There are 11 official languages, however Africans and English are the two most common.

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: South Africa offers the visitor a breathtaking selection of rare species. It’s possible to observe all of the continents’ wild-life within South Africa’s many national parks. Incredible landscapes await us, from the vast planes in the north, to the coastal regions of Cape Town.

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: The two main tribes in South African are the Sotho and the Nguni. Only 15% of the population is of European descent. In Cape Town and the surrounding areas, you can find romantic coves and long white sandy beaches. St. James and Muizenberg

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: still remain relatively untouched. South Africa and sharks. For many visitors these two notions are inseparable. In Cape Town’s huge aquarium, it’s possible to experience these ocean predators first hand. South Africa lies on the southernmost point of the African continent.

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: Bordered by the Indian Ocean on the southern and eastern coasts and the Atlantic on the west coast. We begin our adventure some 5 hours south of Johannesburg – away from civilization. To this day, South Africa still remains a land full of mystery. A country

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: of sorcery and superstition. Firmly rooted in the African culture is the belief in psychic powers. Myths, witches covens and voodoo magic are a part of everyday life for many locals. The ‚Cave of Fertitlity’ is a place where myth and reality converge. This enormous canyon,

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: the largest in the southern hemisphere, lies south of Johannesburg, deep within the Maluti mountain ranges. With a Jeep and our guide Tabeo we set off to discover the secluded cave. After a long and exhaustive drive through

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: almost impassable terrain, we arrive at the area where we believe the cave to be. As Tabeo and I descend, we are approached by the ‚Singoma’, Sabbii. Maybe it’s your dead grandmother or your grandfather that you hear. He comes to you and tells you who he is and where he

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: lives. And he tells you what he wants from you. What he want you to do for him. And you do that you get luck. She tells us, that the ancestral sprits had anticipated our arrival. Sabbii, also says that our ancestral spirits offer advice. I’m supposed to get my misgivings

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: under control, before I’m welcomed into the cave. After the ritualistic cleansing, Sabbii takes us into the ‚Singoma’, realm. The descent into the cave is extremely steep and not without its dangers. There are deadly snakes, which Sabbii describes as the ‚watchmen

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: of the cave’. The ‚Singoma’s, only come to this area when they are called by their ancestors. After a few days, weeks or sometimes even years, they can return once more to their normal lives. This is 43 year old Sabbii’s third time here. She’s earned the title

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: of the ‚High Priestess’, for her ability to converse with the dead. Whilst the witchdoctors slaughters the animal, the participants pray to it for forgiveness, in order to protect themselves and their families. They chant incantations for the soul of the sacrificed animal.

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: The people here believe that once the heart is buried, the soul will go on forever. Here, when you light a candle you can wish for anything you desire.. We are now leaving the mystical world of the north and travelling south towards Cape Town.

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: Cape town. This city is one of the most beautiful metropolises in the world. On the one hand, you have extraordinary mountain ranges, yet nearby lie the white sandy beaches and the surrounding Indian Ocean. Cape Town is an exciting melting pot, containing over 3 million

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: people of diverse race and culture, and is one of South Africa’s major cities. Cape Town’s history began in 1652, with the establishment of a provisions store for the fishermen. ‚Company’s Garden’, is a 5 1/2

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: hectare botanical garden. It is a wonderful area for strolling and offers a shady refuge on hot summer days. One time diamond dealer and Prime Minister of the Cape’s colonies, Cecil Rhodes planted a vegetable garden here to provide the passing fishermen with fresh local produce.

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: The waterfront was the center for fish trading and for small fishing industry. Today, Cape Town’s waterfront is still bordered by two very old docks. Cape Town’s modern harbour is one of its major tourist attractions,

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: but if you want to take a step back in time, come just half an hour out of town with me, to the city of Kalk Bay, where the real fishermen work. 50 years ago, the small fishing village of ‚Kalk Bay’, was known as the ‚small brother’ to Cape Town’s ‚Waterfront’.

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: Amateur fishermen would set sail from here leaving their worries behind. But they soon found themselves in hot demand. These days ‚Kalk Bay’, has a thriving fishing industry supplying many of Cape Town’s top restaurants. The best time to buy fresh fish is strangely enough

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: mid-day. This is when the fishing boots return to the harbor, after the night-shift. The ‚Drum-Kids’, from Cape Town have turned their backs on a life on the streets. They sing and they drum for Africa. ‚Table mountain’, is not only the most prominent landmark in Cape Town,

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: it’s also a symbol of achievement for many adventurers. Well, this is one way to get over a fear of heights. This abseil point is the highest in the world, it’s ‘Table mountain’, in South Africa. Wish me luck. I just don’t look down.

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: ‚Table mountain’, is 1085 meters high and the abseil run is a frightening 113 meters drop. You're going have to see this. This is someone who is really scared of heights. I was okay with the climbing rock part, but when you drop into a sheer nothingness.

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: It took everything for me to get down here. But man, the view was worth it. I tell you, this is incredible. And I did the highest point in the world. I still can’t even move...but I did it.

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: From the top of the mountain to the ocean floor. The underwater world along the south coast of Africa is as incredible as it is diverse. Alongside numerous species of fish, you’ll also find many sharks.

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: And for those of us who’d rather not encounter these predators in the open sea, it’s possible to get get close to them in Cape Town’s aquarium. I’m just going to cover every part of my body that I can, because apparently the sharks are really hungry.

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: Normally these peaceful ‚Grey-Nurse’, sharks, don’t attack humans. However, when provoked, like any animal, they will defend themselves. The ‚Two Oceans Aquarium’ consists of two separate tanks. The ‚Kelp Forest’ tank is the perfect representation of the natural environment

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: found in the Atlantic Ocean. There are over 500 different species of fish living in this underwater world. In the predator’s tank, the sea life is from the warmer Indian Ocean. Apart from sharks, you’ll find ‚Tuna’ an ,Bull rays'. The sharks however, are only fed once a week.

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: That was an experience, a real experience. It was the closest I’ve ever been to a shark. I’d never seen sharks up close before, I’d never seen sharks in the wild before, and these guys were really

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: hungry – twists and turns, but they’re incredibly beautiful creatures. And hey, we’re in their domain, and they put up with us, which was very good. I’m glad. Only one hour from Cape Town lies Boulders Bay. Here you’ll find one of the cutest animals in the world:

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: the Penguin. They live and breed here without a care in the world. When food became scarce at their former habitat on nearby ‚Robin Island’, these glass-eye Penguins relocated to ‚Simon’s Town’. The ‘Cape Peninsula National Park’ at Boulders, is one of the few places in the world

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: where you can get up close and personal with penguins. A few years ago however, the locals faced somewhat of a penguin threat. In search of food, these little, tuxedoed waddles’, trampled without a care through gardens, surrounding houses and even entered private kitchens.

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: Along the renowned ‚Garden Route’ and only a two hour drive east of Cape Town, we arrive in Stellenbosch. It was here about 200 years ago, that the history of South African wine began. ‚Constancia’, is one of the most renowned vineyards

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: in South Africa. The conditions here are ideal for wine cultivation: the climate, soil and its southerly location. The ‚Constancia’ wine tour took us to the traditionally rich vineyards of Buitewerwachting and Zeeivenwacht. A paradise for each and every wine lover.

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: Zeeivenwacht is one of the most picturesque vineyards in this entire region. But Stellenbosch boasts over 40 vineyards, so you can really enjoy a day of wine tasting. Around 300 years ago, Simon van der Steel recognized, that South Africa offered excellent growing conditions,

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: and ample opportunity for successful wine cultivation. The end of ‚apartheid’ in 1990, also marked the end of racial restriction on vineyard ownership. This along with the lifting of the world-wide ban on south African produce, allowed the indigenous people to

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: take part in this flourishing industry. South African wine is now being exported all over the world, to an ever growing number of satisfied customers. The ‚Garden Route’ counts as one of the most beautiful travel routes of the world. It’s 2,500 kilometers long and

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: leads from Cape Town to the east coast of Africa. To this day, ‚Blue Lizard’; the old railway, still transports travelers between, Knysna’ and ,Plettenberg’. A tourist attraction for lovers of nostalgia. The ‚Wilderness National Park’, is a unique recreational

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: area for fans of watersports and for the whole family. A 40 km long creek branches off and travels through a region that is inaccessible by foot. A small excursion through the park can be a wonderful experience. You’ll find 800 varieties of birds and butterflies.

author: wocomoTRAVEL
text: A green oasis on the south coast of Africa. Our journey takes us 100 km’s north-east of the ‚Wilderness Park’. We’re visiting the famous ‚Shamwari Game Reserve’. One of the most beautiful in South Africa.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: When one thinks of South Africa safaris, wine tours and Cape Town may initially come to mind. Although these are some of the biggest draws for tourists the country has so much more to offer.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: Join us as we present to you our 20 day adventure going from Johannesburg all the way to Cape Town. Some of the top travel highlights included a Kruger National Park safari, hiking along the Wild Coast, canoeing in Wilderness and climbing mountains in Northern Drakensberg.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: In terms of our taste buds we tried Biltong for the first time and devoured South African barbecue – better know as Braai – on more than one occasion. There are few countries that are as unique and diverse as South Africa. Come find out what makes it shine. This is South Africa!

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: Our first full day in South Africa started with a long drive from Johannesburg towards Kruger National Park. Even though our safari wouldn't begin until the following day we enjoyed some great views of the countryside and even visited a wildlife rehabilitation center.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: Okay, so we've been driving all morning. We've actually been up since five AM and we are finally stopping for lunch. We're taking a little break. And yeah, we're just going to enjoy a meal before we continue on towards the park. Alright, so our meal has arrived.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: What are we having? We're having a Fred Flinstone portion of ribs. Take a look at this. This is enormous. Ready to dig in! And we're sharing this. There is no way I could eat this all by myself. Especially after being in a bus all day. Alright, Fred Flinstone.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: And the only way to eat these properly is with your hands. Those are pretty good ribs. Okay, so this afternoon we are visiting the Moholoholo Wildlife Rehabilitation Centre. We are going to be visiting some animals and just learning about the rehabilitation effort

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: that is going on here. And yeah, let's go have a look around. Some of the main reasons for rescues include animals being affected by power-lines, snares and also poisoning. It was awesome to see all of the animals but the honey badger truly stole the show.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: Okay, so today is the big day. Yesterday afternoon we reached the outskirts of Kruger National Park and today we're actually going in to spot some of the wildlife. So we're going in for a sunrise drive. It is really earlier. It is about 5:15 am right now.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: We're going to head into the park and - yeah - it is safari time! Okay, so we finally reached the park and I'm going to give you a little tour of our cool truck for the day. Come on over. Come on in. Make that big climb up there. Yeah, I'm in.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: One thing that totally surprised me was just how close we got to the lions. I think it was only about 10 meters away from the road. So they were really docile and the lioness was just sleeping and cleaning its cubs and

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: in the back there was the big alpha male just stretching and yawning. It was surreal. So that's been an amazing safari so far. It is only 10 in the morning and we've already seen more wildlife than I ever imagined. We saw lions, elephants, wildebeest.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: Oh, gosh more than I can even remember. Yeah, it was amazing and we were able to get up close and have a good look at them. So it has been so much fun. So tell us what happened to you at lunchtime today? So the most ridiculous thing happened to me during lunch.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: A bird shit right here. It ricochet of off my shoulder all the way down to here. And while I was still recovering from the shock from that another bird shitted on this side. So I got hit on both shoulders but apparently if a bird shits on you it is good luck.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: So I have a double dose of good luck. Maybe that means I'm going to see cheetahs this afternoon. Woo hoo! Right now we're visiting a private game reserve to see even more cool wildlife. And what caught us for a total surprise is that we saw so many giraffes.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: Apparently this is the most giraffes have ever been spotted out here. So we saw them walk right in front of our vehicle and we even saw a couple of them fighting. They were swinging their heads at each other. It was completely bonkers. Yeah, so overall it has been an amazing day.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: We saw more animals than we ever expected. And what a beautiful way to wrap up the day. Now we're just going to watch the sunset, drink some wine and enjoy some cheese. And do it all over again tomorrow.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: Okay, so it is day two on safari and we are heading back to Kruger National Park. The focus today is going to be to find animals like being around the water. So we're going to be visiting a watering hole maybe seeing some buffalo, some hippos and we'll see what else we can find.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: So again, it's an early start. It's around five thirty in the morning and we're going to head on out there. So far, so good as far as the weather today. It looks like we're going to have a lot better visibility. We had an amazing sunrise to witness this morning.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: But it is chilly and so we're all bundled up. It's a lot colder than it was yesterday but the sun is coming out. We actually had a little bit of drizzle yesterday, so it'll be warmer. Just waiting for it to get warmer.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: And I think we're going to have even better visibility for photos and video in terms of taking shots of the animals. So, exciting stuff! Okay, so a pretty cool morning so far. We managed to spot the ever elusive leopard. So that was pretty cool.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: And we also saw a heard of elephants that were just crossing the road. There was about fifteen or twenty of them, so that was also a pretty impressive sight. One of the coolest things that I've seen today was at a watering hole.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: There was a crocodile and it was waiting for an impala to drink. And it was so close to attacking it. Like we almost saw this ridiculous crazy scene. Anyways, the crocodile was unsuccessful and it ended up attacking another crocodile. And we captured a bit of that footage.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: One safari tip to consider is to dress with all kinds of layers. This morning it was freezing. I had on a fleece, I had on a windbreaker and now by the afternoon I'm peeling down to just this tiny kind of athletic shirt. And you've got thick jogging pants on.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: Yeah, so I wish I had shorts right now for that. Alright, so we are wrapping up our second day of safari. We're going to be heading to the campgrounds soon and we're going to have our first braai for dinner and apparently that is South African barbecue so it should be tasty.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: Alright, so this morning we are in Drakensberg. That means the dragon's mountain. And we're going to be doing a bit of a hike on a stretch that is called the Amphitheatre. And yeah, the Drakensberg are actually the border between South Africa and Lesotho.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: So that is a pretty cool little fact. We've been told to prepare for any kind of weather. It could be sunny. It could be rainy. It could be snowing any minute. So we're ready for some adventure. Let's go hiking! I've really been enjoying the views so far.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: I would classify this as a lower-intermediate hike. It is not for people who really have a fear of heights. There are some vertical parts where you have to go up ladders and down ladders. And that part would be a deal breaker for some people.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: But as far as the views and just the actual hike itself it is really enjoyable. So this is actually one of the ladders that you have to climb down on the way back. And I believe there are two sets of ladders to deal with. So something to consider if you are afraid of heights.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: We've actually decided to just stay back and enjoy a very relaxed lunch because neither Sam nor I enjoy heights and this was making us a little bit nervous. But everyone else was very adventurous, very brave so you can give it a try if you come out here.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: Okay, so now it is time for lunch. Woo! Alright, so we are coming to the end of our hike here in Drakensberg. And even though we didn't reach the top it was still an incredible trip. Like it was so scenic, so beautiful, so I'm glad we came.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: And if you find yourself traveling through South Africa definitely consider this region. So, good morning. Today we are in Northern Drakensberg. We've been staying at a really cool hostel called Amphitheater backpackers.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: And they have a little hike that you can do just outside of their property. So we're just over at the gate right now. We're going to head out and do a little bit of exploring on foot. It's a beautiful morning. We witnessed the most amazing sunrise yet again.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: And now it is time for a bit of exercise. We're at a crossroads over here. Big tree this way. Big Dam that way. Oh, which way will we go? Okay, this way. It is decided. Big tree. This is like falling the yellow brick road. Come on, sing it for us. Follow the yellow brick road.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: Follow the yellow brick road. So we are at an another crossroads. We can go towards the airstrip or towards the river. I think we're going to choose the river just because it is starting to get really hot. I've already started taking off several layers.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: And we're running out of time. And some water would be nice. I really enjoyed this walk. Sometimes it is great to just get off of the beaten track once in a while and just relax. And that concludes our little walk around. I think it is time for a cold beer.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: Okay, so a new country, a new taste test. We are in South Africa right now and you can't come to this country and not try Biltong. So that is what I have right here in this brown paper bag. So Biltong is a dried cured meat similar to beef jerky and this is what it looks like.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: So we got ourselves some beef biltong but apparently you can get different kinds of meats. Like game meat or ostrich. This is cow. And it is quite hard and chewy but it makes a really nice snack, so let's say you're riding

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: the bus or going on a really long trip it is kind of nice to just have a bag full and be munching away. That's what we have been doing for the past few days. So for me Biltong is kind of like premium jerky. And there is three key differences.

author: Nomadic Samuel - Travel Channel
text: First off, it is sliced a lot thicker. Secondly, they use vinegar in the curing process along with salt and pepper. Which is a little bit different from regular jerky which doesn't have any vinegar whatsoever.

text: Blind River Canyon is a must visit for nature lovers. It runs for 16 mi and the landscape is well, the pictures speak for themselves. With sheer drops of up to 800 meters, the blind river canyon offers some of the most spectacular views in the entire country. Hikers

author: MostAmazingTop10
text: often make the journey to visit what's known as the pinnacle, a column of solid quartzite rising up out of the woods that contain some of the most diverse plants and animals in the world. It's incredible stuff. But if any of you guys don't really like natural canyons and

author: MostAmazingTop10
text: you like man-made stuff, then how about a massive man-made hole? Because our number two is just that, and it's just called Big Hole. also known as Kimberly. The big hole was nothing more than a hill back in 1871. But then someone found diamonds there and everything

author: MostAmazingTop10
text: changed. By the following year, the mining camp had 50,000 people living there. And for the next 43 years, they used hand tools to dig a hole that displaced 22 million tons of earth, leaving a hole that was 214 m deep and had a perimeter of 1.6 km. To this day,

author: MostAmazingTop10
text: it claims to be the largest hole excavated by human hands alone. So, they must have found a lot of diamonds, right guys? Well, apparently not. As the sign that is actually there points out, all the diamonds that were ever dug up from the ground there would only be enough to

author: MostAmazingTop10
text: fill up just three mining carts. What? And now at number one, we got the city of Johannesburg. Also known as Joberg or Josie. This city is the largest in South Africa with a population of 3.2 million and growing fast. It's often referred to as the city of gold because it was

author: MostAmazingTop10
text: established based on the discovery of gold on a nearby farm way back in 1886. Many tourists are drawn to the city to experience its clash of the old and the new. Its many museums such as the Apartheid Museum or the Origins Museum document the city and the country's near

author: MostAmazingTop10
text: and distant history. But with such a rapidly developing and growing city, it's no surprise there are plenty of more modern attractions. These range from grand shopping malls, a vibrant night life, and nearby safaris to name but a few. But just like everything on

author: MostAmazingTop10
text: this list that we finished, now you just have to go and see it for yourself to really know what it's all about. And that's all we got time for, guys. And now I've got two questions for you. If you're from South Africa, then did I miss anything out on the list? Or if

author: MostAmazingTop10
